response:
The fare $\eta$ depends on the driving distance $\xi$ with a base of ten yuan for the first four kilometers. Any distance beyond four kilometers adds two yuan per kilometer to the total cost. This relationship means the spring of traffic can cause the distance to increase significantly. The formula connects the random variable for distance directly to the random variable for fare.

A passenger paying thirty-eight yuan implies a calculated distance of eighteen kilometers based on the fare rules. Since the actual road distance is fifteen kilometers, the extra three kilometers must come from waiting time. Each kilometer of equivalent distance represents five minutes of stopping time in this city. Therefore the maximum stop time is fifteen minutes which equals three kilometers. This calculation shows how the spring of waiting affects the final price. The driver must minimize stops to keep the fare lower for passengers.
